sections.title: Phrasal verbs
items: **hang up** — put clothes in a wardrobe, etc
**pick up** — lift something from the floor, a table, etc
**put back** — return something to where it was
**share out** — give a part of sth to a group of people
**work out** — find the solution to a problem, etc

exercises.title: Topic vocabulary (gap fill)
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences with words from the box.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences with words from the box.

items.sentence: The headteacher will ...... the complaint before speaking to the students.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is look into a problem, so the correct answer is "investigate".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: You shouldn't ...... that the problem is easy to solve.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is think without proof, so the correct answer is "assume".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: He made a ...... that the machine was faulty.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun after made a, so the correct answer is "claim".

exercises.title: Circle the correct word
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct option in each sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the correct option in each sentence.

items.sentenceBefore: In my ......, we should ask for help.
items.originalSentence: In my ......, we should ask for help. **view / pressure**
items.completedSentence: In my ......, we should ask for help. view

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "view" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "view" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "pressure" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.pressure: "pressure" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.pressure: "pressure" doesn't fit here → "view" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

items.originalSentence: His bedroom is always ...... . **in a mess / in danger**
items.completedSentence: His bedroom is always ...... . in a mess

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "in a mess" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "in a mess" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "in danger" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.in danger: "in danger" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.in danger: "in danger" doesn't fit here → "in a mess" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

items.sentenceBefore: Don't believe every ...... you hear at school.
items.originalSentence: Don't believe every ...... you hear at school. **discussion / rumour**
items.completedSentence: Don't believe every ...... you hear at school. rumour

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "rumour" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "rumour" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "discussion" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.discussion: "discussion" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.discussion: "discussion" doesn't fit here → "rumour" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

items.sentenceBefore: She makes good decisions when she is ...... .
items.originalSentence: She makes good decisions when she is ...... . **by accident / under pressure**
items.completedSentence: She makes good decisions when she is ...... . under pressure

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "under pressure" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "under pressure" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "by accident" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.by accident: "by accident" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.by accident: "by accident" doesn't fit here → "under pressure" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

items.sentenceBefore: The coach ...... the players after they won the match.
items.originalSentence: The coach ...... the players after they won the match. **criticised / praised**
items.completedSentence: The coach ...... the players after they won the match. praised

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "praised" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

wrongOptions.criticised: "criticised" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

optionContrast.criticised: "criticised" doesn't fit here → "praised" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

items.sentenceBefore: I sent the message to the wrong person ...... .
items.originalSentence: I sent the message to the wrong person ...... . **in trouble / by mistake**
items.completedSentence: I sent the message to the wrong person ...... . by mistake

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "by mistake" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "by mistake" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "in trouble" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.in trouble: "in trouble" doesn't fit here → "by mistake" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

exercises.title: Phrasal verbs (complete the gaps)
exercises.instructions: Write the correct phrasal verb in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write the correct phrasal verb in each gap.

items.sentence: Please ...... the papers from the floor.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is collect from the floor, so the correct answer is "pick up".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "pick up", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: We must ...... this misunderstanding today.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is solve a problem, so the correct answer is "sort out".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "sort out", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "sort out" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

items.sentence: ......! The floor is wet.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is warning, so the correct answer is "Watch out".

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: We might ...... time if we don't start soon.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is no more left, so the correct answer is "run out of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "run out of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Comparatives and superlatives compare people, things, or actions.

items.sentence: The teacher asked us to ...... the books equally.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is distribute equally, so the correct answer is "share out".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "share out", not just one word.

explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "share out" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

exercises.title: Word patterns

items.sentence: She hid the bad news ...... her parents.

exercises.title: Word formation
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct form of the word in capitals.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with the correct form of the word in capitals.

items.sentence: Can you give me some ...... about this problem? (ADVICE)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is uncountable noun, so the correct answer is "advice".

wrongOptions.advice: The root ADVICE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "advice".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of ADVICE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: ADVICE → advice: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "advice" is the word-formation answer from the root ADVICE in this context.

items.sentence: The map was confusing, so we all felt ...... . (CONFUSE)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is how you feel, so the correct answer is "confused".

wrongOptions.confuse: The root CONFUSE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "confused".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of CONFUSE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: CONFUSE → confused: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "confused" is the word-formation answer from the root CONFUSE in this context.

items.sentence: There is one ...... to this rule. (EXCEPT)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from EXCEPT, so the correct answer is "exception".

wrongOptions.except: The root EXCEPT cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "exception".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of EXCEPT that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: EXCEPT → exception: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "exception" is the word-formation answer from the root EXCEPT in this context.

items.sentence: Your comments were very ...... . (HELP)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is adjective, so the correct answer is "helpful".

wrongOptions.help: The root HELP cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "helpful".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of HELP that fits grammatically, not just the root.

items.sentenceBefore: If people talk about something that may not be true, it is a

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "rumour" — unverified story.

wrongOptions.A: "theory" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "rumour" is correct.
wrongOptions.theory: "theory" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "rumour" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "purpose" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "rumour" is correct.
wrongOptions.purpose: "purpose" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "rumour" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "thought" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "rumour" is correct.

items.sentence: It's unkind to ...... about someone behind their back.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is talk behind backs, so the correct answer is "gossip".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.
